It also removes the postinstall script and deletes mr_robot.js to hide activity. These actions clearly attempt to modify ownership of other npm packages (tampering) and grant the attacker account owner access (enabling remote control of those packages). The behavior is triggered at install time via the postinstall hook. Therefore the package is malicious. | The package's package.json defines a postinstall script that runs mr_robot.js at install time. The mr_robot.js file immediately daemonizes the process (require('daemon')()), which spawns a detached child and exits the parent â€?a common persistence/stealth technique. The file requires child_process.exec (enabling arbitrary shell commands), request and cheerio (enabling network calls and scraping), and defines functions named addOwner, getModulesOwned, removePostInstall, and removeScript, indicating capabilities to change npm package ownership and to remove or alter install scripts (system tampering and creating a backdoor). These concrete artifacts (install-time execution + code that can alter package ownership and execute commands) provide strong evidence of malicious intent and functionality triggered at install time. | The package defines a postinstall script (package.json: postinstall: node ./mr_robot.js) which causes mr_robot.js to run during installation. mr_robot.js immediately daemonizes (require('daemon')()), uses child_process.exec, and imports network libraries (request, cheerio). annotation.reasoning: The mr_robot.js file contains explicit, concrete malicious actions: it executes 'npm whoami' to determine the npm user, requests https://www.npmjs.org/~<user> to enumerate packages, and then runs 'npm owner add mr_robot <package>' for each packageâ€”this directly tampers with npm package ownership enabling attacker takeover. It also removes the package.json postinstall script and attempts to delete mr_robot.js to hide traces, and daemonizes to persist background operation. Those behaviors indicate active tampering of projects and creation of a backdoor into packages. The pattern (removing postinstall, self-delete) and use of exec imply installation-time payload execution, so trigger_type is 'install_time'. | The provided mr_robot.js contains concrete code patterns showing malicious capabilities: require('daemon')() at top-level will background the process; use of child_process.exec enables arbitrary command execution; request + cheerio indicate network fetching and scraping likely used to discover targets; explicit function names (addOwner, getModulesOwned, removePostInstall, removeScript, currentUser) strongly suggest attempts to enumerate and alter npm package ownership and install scripts. These are active, harmful behaviors (remote command execution/backdoor and modification of package metadata/scripts), so the package is classified as malicious with BACKDOOR_CONTROL and SYSTEM_TAMPERER capabilities. Triggering appears to occur at module load/runtime because the daemonize call is at top-level. | The provided mr_robot.js includes a top-level call to require('daemon')() which will fork and background the process when the file is loaded. The file also requires child_process.exec, request, fs and defines clearly named functions such as addOwner, getModulesOwned, currentUser, removePostInstall and removeScript. These function names and the presence of network and shell capabilities are concrete indicators of code designed to take control over npm package ownership, execute commands, and modify installation scripts (persistence/cleanup). This combination constitutes malicious behavior: BACKDOOR_CONTROL (gaining remote/privileged control over packages) and SYSTEM_TAMPERER (modifying package install scripts/owners). The package.json lifecycle scripts were not provided, so the exact trigger timing (install vs runtime) cannot be determined from the supplied files alone. | The provided mr_robot.js bundle includes explicit calls to require('child_process').exec and require('daemon')(), indicating background shell execution. It also requires HTTP fetching and HTML parsing modules (request, cheerio) and defines functions named currentUser, addOwner, getModulesOwned, removePostInstall, and removeScript. These function names and capabilities strongly suggest attempts to enumerate/npm-user-owned modules, add owners (potentially to take over packages), and remove postinstall scripts to hide traces â€?actions that modify package metadata and repository ownership (SYSTEM_TAMPERER). No safe/benign functionality or only-library code explains these behaviors, and no evidence was found that would limit this to benign use. There is no clear evidence of credential exfiltration, remote command-and-control, or resource abuse in the provided snippet, so only SYSTEM_TAMPERER is reported. | The provided static content includes a large JavaScript file that at top-level requires child_process.exec and immediately calls require('daemon')(), which will daemonize the process. It also requires request and cheerio (used for web scraping) and defines functions with names that strongly suggest malicious package manipulation (currentUser, getModulesOwned, addOwner, removePostInstall, removeScript). Those indicate capabilities to enumerate packages owned by a user, add an owner (potentially the attacker) to packages, and remove postinstall scripts to hide changes or prevent remediation. The use of exec gives the code the ability to run arbitrary shell commands. Although no explicit network exfiltration or C2 domains are visible in the provided snippet, the combination of background execution, shell execution, and functions for altering package ownership and scripts is concrete evidence of malicious behavior (remote control/persistence and tampering). The exact trigger moment (install-time vs runtime) is not explicit from the provided files, so trigger_type is set to unknown.

Additionally, an included module executes a shell command that reads and base64-encodes /etc/passwd, /etc/hosts and /etc/shadow (if accessible) and sends them to a remote domain, demonstrating explicit data theft of system credentials and files. These concrete actions constitute malicious data-stealing behavior and tracking; therefore the package is malicious.
